problem_statement: AI programming tools exclude users from decision-making, causing over-reliance on and blind trust in a single 'best' completion, which leads programmers to unwittingly accept insecure code; the paper addresses how to keep humans meaningfully in the loop during LLM code generation.
magnitude_evidence: studies have found that programmers write less secure code with AI assistants than without [5]-[7]; in the study, BASELINE participants generated 31% more vulnerabilities (MHiLDe=2.67, MBASELINE=3.89, p=0.01)
insufficiency_evidence: Vasconcelos et al. found that decoding uncertainty highlighting alone offered no advantage over a baseline without it; 'Every solution written with BASELINE used insecure password hashing practices' and BASELINE only suggested SHA256 without salting
solution_mechanisms: natural language explanations of alternative differences
corrected-entropy uncertainty highlighting using semantic importance
completion regeneration preserving similarity to base
analysis LLM scoring alternatives (Significant/Minor/Incorrect + importance score)
